demographics.living_situation: resident in a residential aged-care facility (nursing home); fully dependent on staff for care

contact_nok.relationship: facility registered nurse on duty (calling on the resident's behalf)

opening_complaint.verbatim_patient_text: [Facility RN calling] He has been incredibly thirsty for days and urinating constantly, and today he doesn't even know his own name and can barely open his eyes.
opening_complaint.stated_reason_for_presenting_today: The resident has become much more confused and drowsy through today after a week of heavy thirst and passing lots of urine, and the staff are worried something is badly wrong.

symptom_narrative.onset: came on gradually over the last 5 to 7 days
symptom_narrative.duration: about a week, and much worse today
symptom_narrative.character: very thirsty and drinking constantly with lots of urine earlier in the week; over the last day or two he has become extremely drowsy, weak and confused
symptom_narrative.severity_patient_description: the nurse says this is the worst she has seen him — barely rousable and he doesn't know his own name
symptom_narrative.timing_pattern: thirst and heavy urination first, then steadily increasing drowsiness and confusion
symptom_narrative.what_makes_it_worse: he has been too lethargic to drink much or take his tablets for the last few days
symptom_narrative.associated_symptoms_volunteered: no vomiting, no tummy pain, and staff have not noticed any fast or heavy breathing; his urine smells very strong and concentrated
symptom_narrative.previous_episodes: nothing like this has happened to him before
symptom_narrative.functional_impact: now bedbound and unable to feed or drink by himself

social_history_volunteered.relevant_life_context: lives in a residential aged-care facility; fully dependent for care; nursing staff are present and made the call on his behalf

objective_data_offered.value: the glucometer just reads 'HI' — it won't give a number
objective_data_offered.reliability_caveat: facility fingerstick meter; 'HI' means the glucose is above the meter's readable limit, not an exact value
single reading taken by facility nursing staff on a ward cuff

objective_data_offered.value: he looks gaunt and drawn with deeply sunken eyes and dry, cracked lips; he is very drowsy and only half-opens his eyes
when the nurse pinches the skin on his arm it stays tented for a few seconds before flattening; his mouth and tongue look bone dry
several empty water jugs around the bed and a strong smell of concentrated urine in the room
